title: How’s the economic well-being of U.S. households?
gist: Drawing on the Federal Reserve's 2025 Survey of Household Economics and Decisionmaking, the FRED Blog shows that 73% of U.S. adults reported being at least okay financially, with higher educational attainment consistently linked to greater financial comfort, while low-income, young, and Black adults saw meaningful declines in well-being.

insights.claim: In the 2025 Survey of Household Economics and Decisionmaking (SHED), 73% of U.S. adults reported being okay or comfortable in their financial conditions.
insights.speaker: St. Louis Fed — FRED Blog
insights.so_what: Provides a baseline gauge of consumer financial health relevant to spending and credit outlooks.
insights.quote: In the 2025 SHED, 73% of adults reported okay or comfortable financial conditions.

insights.claim: The Federal Reserve Board has conducted the Survey of Household Economics and Decisionmaking since 2013 to measure household economic well-being and identify potential risks to household finances.
insights.speaker: St. Louis Fed — FRED Blog
insights.so_what: A long-running official series offering a consistent read on household financial fragility, credit access, savings, and retirement.
insights.quote: Since 2013, the Federal Reserve Board has conducted the SHED—the Survey of Household Economics and Decisionmaking.

insights.claim: Higher levels of formal education are consistently associated with higher reports of comfortable living conditions among U.S. adults.
insights.speaker: St. Louis Fed — FRED Blog
insights.so_what: Education-linked income divergence reinforces a persistent driver of consumer-segment inequality.
insights.quote: Higher levels of formal education are consistently associated with higher reports of comfortable living conditions.

insights.claim: More education generally means more human capital and income, which results in a more positive overall financial self-assessment.
insights.speaker: St. Louis Fed — FRED Blog
insights.so_what: Frames the mechanism by which the education gap translates into divergent consumer financial resilience.
insights.quote: More education generally means more human capital and income, which results in a more positive overall financial self-assessment.

insights.claim: Certain demographic groups, including low-income, young, and Black adults, saw meaningful declines in their economic well-being in the 2025 SHED.
insights.speaker: St. Louis Fed — FRED Blog
insights.so_what: Pockets of deteriorating well-being signal uneven consumer strength beneath a stable aggregate, relevant to credit and discretionary demand.
insights.quote: certain demographic groups, including low-income, young, and Black adults, saw meaningful declines in their economic well-being
